narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.70—well above the typical 3.21 average—your law school application stood out in some academic areas, but the admissions model ultimately predicted a 56.5% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, tipping the decision toward rejection. This prediction draws from patterns in data on thousands of past students, focusing on factors like early grades, later academic standing, test scores, and background details such as your middle-income family bracket and plans to attend full-time, which is the norm for over 92% of students. The model's call hinged most heavily on three key elements of your profile: your undergraduate GPA, your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2 (below the average of 5.53), and your Year 1 decile of 1 (far below the typical 5.71). Your strong undergrad GPA was the biggest positive force, strongly pushing the prediction toward expecting you to pass the bar by showing you have the academic foundation many struggle with. However, your lower decile in Year 3 had the strongest pull in the opposite direction, signaling to the model a dip in performance relative to peers that often correlates with bar struggles. Your even lower Year 1 decile reinforced this concern, though to a lesser extent, highlighting early challenges that the model sees as persistent risks compared to students who start stronger. Despite your LSAT score of 28 being below the average of 36.70, it wasn't among the top influencers here. Overall, while your GPA gives you an edge over typical applicants, the model weighed your weaker school deciles as bigger red flags, placing you in a higher-risk group for first-time bar failure. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a full-time student from a middle-income background—it's just how these patterns played out for you specifically. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score toward the average, strengthening your later-year grades to climb those deciles, or gathering letters highlighting your undergrad success and resilience. You might also explore programs with holistic reviews or prep courses proven to lift bar passage rates for profiles like yours. With targeted improvements, you can shift the odds and reapply stronger next cycle.
